title: Reset Linux Root Password via Grub Menu
summary: This skill describes how to reset a forgotten Linux root password by booting into single-user mode through the Grub menu.

root_steps.title: Press E at the Grub menu
root_steps.description: The Grub menu appears right after you turn on the computer. In most cases, it only stays on the screen for a few moments.

root_steps.title: Scroll to the line that begins with linux /boot
root_steps.description: Use the ↑ and ↓ keys to do so. This is the line you will need to modify in order to boot into single-user mode.

root_steps.title: Move the cursor to the end of the line
root_steps.description: Use the →, ←, ↑, and ↓ keys to place the cursor right after ro.

root_steps.title: Type init=/bin/bash after ro
root_steps.description: The end of the line should now look like this: ro init=/bin/bash. Note the space between ro and init=/bin/bash.

root_steps.title: Press Ctrl+X
root_steps.description: This tells the system to boot directly to a root-level command prompt in single-user mode.

root_steps.title: Type mount –o remount,rw / at the prompt
root_steps.description: This mounts the file system in read-write mode.

root_steps.title: Type passwd at the prompt
root_steps.description: Since booting into single-user mode gives you root access, there is no need to pass additional parameters to the passwd command.

root_steps.title: Type a new root password
root_steps.description: The characters you type will not be displayed on the screen. This is normal.

root_steps.title: Retype the new password
root_steps.description: When the system confirms you’ve re-entered the same password, you will see a message that reads “password updated successfully.”

root_steps.title: Type reboot –f and press ↵ Enter
root_steps.description: This command reboots the system normally.
